sessions.name: Chathura Ratnayake
sessions.title: CDO
sessions.focus: Full org ownership, execution model, sprint reality, support unification
topics: AI strategy from the build side
sessions.lead_with: I've pulled together data across all your teams -- sprint velocity trends, epic completion rates, reviewer bottlenecks, CI/CD coverage by workspace. Some of it might be useful as you're ramping into the CDO role.
sessions.what_they_get: The full systems picture he hasn't had time to build. Sprint velocity trends, epic completion rates, reviewer bottlenecks, CI/CD coverage by workspace -- assembled across all his teams in one view.
sessions.arc_notes: Most important interview. Morning freshness for both sides. He's 10 days into CDO -- use 'how are you figuring it out?' framing alongside data probes. Part 1 of 2; follow-up Wednesday W8.
top_questions: Sprint velocity on the Ops Prime board dropped from 127 to 56 completed issues per sprint across Q1 -- a 56% decline. The OS board shows a similar pattern. What happened in Q1?
There are 18,583 open Jira issues across the org -- 13,725 in engineering, 66% older than a year. Is the backlog actively groomed, or has it become a place where tickets go to die?
Over 26 weeks, engineering created 4,701 issues and resolved 4,432 -- net growth of 269. Is anyone tracking that trend?
The madpayments workspace operates very differently from the rest -- TypeScript, 87% CI/CD, EKS-native, zero IAM users. Was that intentional? Could it be a model for other teams?

scheduling.why: Owns all engineering, product, PMO, and customer support (~100+ people). No substitute.

sessions.notes: Part 1 of 2. Follow-up session W8 on Wednesday.

sessions.name: Jack Kennedy
sessions.title: CTO
sessions.focus: AI/innovation, Neo platform, Winmark delivery
sessions.lead_with: I'm genuinely curious about the Winmark build -- an AI-developed POS for 1,265 franchise stores is a significant technical challenge. Walk me through how you're approaching it.
sessions.what_they_get: Genuine interest in what he's building. Not the org chart changes, not what he lost -- what he's creating right now and what he's learning from agentic programming at scale.
sessions.arc_notes: Handle with care. Operational scope just removed in April 3 reorg. Focus on what he's building, not what he lost. Don't probe the reorg dynamics directly -- let him volunteer. Lead with curiosity about Winmark.
top_questions: I've looked across all 18 AWS accounts and can't find a dedicated Neo/AI production environment. I see SageMaker artifacts in R&D and active Jira projects, but where does the AI platform actually run in production?
83% of running EC2 instances are on pre-Graviton hardware, and 21% of Lambda functions are on end-of-life runtimes. Is there a modernization initiative?
The Shared Services account runs a full Grafana stack -- Mimir, Loki, Tempo. How broadly adopted is it across teams?
3,191 repos across four Bitbucket workspaces, 75% stale. Is there a repo lifecycle policy?

relevant_pages.label: AWS accounts, SageMaker, no Neo account

scheduling.why: Only person who can speak to AI/Neo strategy and the Winmark delivery. No substitute.

sessions.notes: Narrowed scope post-reorg. Currently full-time on Winmark client delivery ($5M). Reduced from 60 min.

sessions.name: Dulanjan Wengappuliarachchi
sessions.title: VP, Product & GTM
sessions.focus: Roadmap governance, product decisions, PM/eng handoff
sessions.lead_with: The documented prioritization process -- multi-department scoring, exec alignment on the top 5, ARB-approved architecture before engineering begins -- is more structured than most companies I've seen. I want to understand how it works in practice.
sessions.what_they_get: Validation that the process he's built is solid -- and a candid conversation about where it may break under pressure from sales or executive overrides.
sessions.arc_notes: Reduced from 60 min. Focus on the prioritization process and whether it actually holds under pressure. Don confirmed Retail and Neo/AI are in scope -- ask about those roadmaps.
top_questions: Every major retail customer has their own Jira project -- Brooks Brothers, Ralph Lauren, Estee Lauder. How do you roll up a portfolio view across 20+ customer projects?
In the last 30 days across all of Jira, 916 issues created but only 658 resolved. How do you decide what gets deprioritized?
How often does a customer escalation or sales promise override the prioritization process mid-sprint?
You have 6 PMs, a design director, an open marketing role, and L&D. That's a broad scope. What suffers most from that breadth right now?

scheduling.why: Only product leadership -- owns all PMs, design, product marketing.
alternatives: Miru S. (Sr PM, Restaurant) could cover product process but not the VP perspective

sessions.notes: Reduced from 60 min. Don confirmed Retail and Neo/AI in scope -- ask about Concierge and Neo roadmaps.

sessions.name: David Strainick
sessions.title: COO
sessions.focus: Account management, onboarding pipeline, IT governance, customer ops chain
sessions.lead_with: I've been mapping the customer operations chain -- signed contract to live deployment -- and I'd love to validate it with you. You own the full pipeline now: Account Management, Onboarding, Delivery, and IT.
sessions.what_they_get: A process map of the operations chain he may not have assembled himself yet, 10 days into a completely new role scope.
sessions.arc_notes: 10 days into COO role (was CPO). Completely different interview than originally planned. Use 'how are you figuring it out?' framing. Watch for whether he has command of the operational details or is still discovering them -- both are valid data.
top_questions: Walk me through how a new customer goes from signed contract to live deployment. Where does it break?
IT reports to you now, not the CTO. Jorge Maltes handles PCI compliance, Rosen Georgiev handles day-to-day ops. How do you govern IT priorities across both?
Sri Lanka's country head reports to you. Engineering resources there have dotted lines to Chathura/Zubair. How does that work in practice?

The 90-day plan flags backlog onboarding as the #1 execution risk -- HIGH likelihood, HIGH impact. I see 2,500+ open customer implementation tickets in Jira, 77% older than a year. What's the technology bottleneck?

scheduling.why: Only person who owns the full customer operations chain (Account Mgmt, Onboarding, Delivery, IT). No substitute.

sessions.notes: Was CPO, now COO. Completely different interview than originally planned.

sessions.name: Manuel Garcia
sessions.title: Interim CFO
sessions.org_size: Mark Do (Controller), Zach Honnold (RevOps), Andy Honnold (Payments Strategy)
sessions.focus: Financial model, vendor spend, cost reduction targets
sessions.lead_with: I've been cross-referencing the AP data from the financial model against the vendor spend spreadsheets Ana shared. A few things jumped out that I'd like to validate with you.
sessions.what_they_get: A technology-informed perspective on which cost cuts are safe vs. risky. The $50K/month software savings target needs someone who understands what the tools actually do.
sessions.arc_notes: New to exec team. 30 min is sufficient -- focused questions on cost structure and vendor relationships. He owns Finance and RevOps, absorbed from Siegel.
top_questions: The 90-day plan commits to $50K/month in software cost reductions starting June and $50K/month in AWS reductions. Where is the low-hanging fruit vs. what requires engineering work to consolidate?
AP is stretching 300+ days for some vendors -- BDO at 309 days, LinkedIn at 203 days. How does that affect vendor relationships and negotiating leverage on renewals?
Revenue Operations moved to you from Siegel. How is that transition going? What are you inheriting?

scheduling.why: Financial model and vendor spend perspective. New Interim CFO.
alternatives: Can skip if unavailable -- financial data already captured from the 90-day plan

sessions.notes: New to exec team. Absorbed RevOps from Siegel. Financial model questions from the plan.

sessions.name: Mark Guilarte
sessions.title: VP Program Management
sessions.focus: PMO integration with engineering sprints, cross-team coordination
sessions.lead_with: Your PMO domain assignments map 1:1 to the product lines -- Qaiser on Restaurant, Vanessa on Payments, Ian on AI, Debbie on Ops. That's a clean structure.
sessions.what_they_get: Recognition that his PMO model is logically organized and an important coordination layer.
sessions.arc_notes: 30 min is sufficient -- focused questions on coordination mechanics.
top_questions: Your team has one PM per domain -- 4 people coordinating across 100+ engineers. How do you maintain visibility across all those teams?

The product roadmap shows 12+ ongoing CAKE projects, 4+ Payments integrations, and Engineering platform work. How does the GTM tracker work in practice? Does it drive the 4-week release cadence, or do teams ship independently?
Ian is the Project Lead for AI. What does that portfolio look like day-to-day? Which AI/Neo projects are active?

scheduling.why: PMO coordination across all product lines.

sessions.notes: Moved to Monday to free Tuesday for deeper engineering sessions.

sessions.name: Alexander Baine
sessions.title: Manager, Software Engineering (Restaurant Frontend)
sessions.focus: Sprint reality, blockers, ground-level CAKE perspective
sessions.lead_with: Adapt based on their team's specific data from velocity and PR metrics. Lead with a specific observation about their board or repo.
sessions.what_they_get: A voice to the CEO they've never had. The universal closing question gives them direct input to Don.
sessions.arc_notes: Use EM interview templates. Look up their team's board in velocity data and repos in PR metrics before the session. Moved to Monday afternoon so Tuesday morning VP answers from Randy are fresh for cross-reference.
top_questions: Your board [NAME] shows [TREND] over the last N sprints -- [VALUES]. Does that match your experience? What drove it?
Your team's average PR merge time on [REPO] is X hours. Does that feel right, or are there outliers skewing it?
What percentage of your sprint goes to planned work versus interrupt-driven work -- escalations, bugs, support?
When priorities change mid-sprint, how does your team hear about it?

scheduling.why: CAKE engineering manager who runs sprints day-to-day. Confirmed by Chathura.

sessions.notes: Confirmed by Chathura (added by CR). Restaurant Frontend Manager under Randy Brown. Manages Cory Renard (Staff) and Rob Quin (SWE). Look up frontend-specific repos (react-cinco, etc.) and PR cycle times before session. Frontend team of 3 for 15+ live CAKE UI surfaces.

sessions.name: Randy Brown
sessions.title: VP Eng, Restaurant Technology
sessions.focus: CAKE capacity vs. surface area, V3/V4 coexistence, outage patterns
topics: QE reporting to Payments not Restaurant
sessions.lead_with: The data confirms what you probably already know -- your team is carrying a lot. 9 engineers for 15+ live systems plus 6+ in active development is an extraordinary ratio.
sessions.what_they_get: Objective, data-backed validation that his team is under-resourced relative to the system surface area. He's probably been saying this internally -- now there's external evidence.
sessions.arc_notes: Lead with respect for what his team manages on thin resources. Ask him to recommend which EM or IC to talk to on Tuesday afternoon.
top_questions: The REST Jira project has 17,318 issues -- more than the next four projects combined. How much of that is actively worked versus inherited backlog?
The syscolabs workspace has 1,527 repos, but 76% haven't been touched in 90 days. The QA project alone has 222 repos. Is there a plan to archive or consolidate?
PR cycle time for cake-payment-gateway averages 78 hours. react-cinco averages 31 hours. Are these the same team? What drives the difference?
I found Jenkins servers running in the CAKE Development AWS account. Are you on Jenkins, Bitbucket Pipelines, or both? Is there a consolidation plan?

relevant_pages.label: CAKE repos, PR cycle times, CI/CD
CAKE Dev AWS account, Jenkins instances

scheduling.why: Owns all CAKE/Restaurant engineering (10 people, 15+ live systems). No substitute.

sessions.notes: Ask him who from his team (Alexander Baine or Kyle Budd) would be best to talk to in M7.

sessions.name: Zubair Syed
sessions.title: VP Eng, Enterprise Solutions
sessions.focus: Concierge/Retail execution, enterprise customers, cross-workspace allocation
sessions.lead_with: You have the largest engineering team in the company and I have the least data on it -- your section of the CDO org chart was mostly blank. I want to hear directly from you how your org is structured and what you're focused on.
sessions.what_they_get: Being heard and made visible. His org was literally blank on the CDO chart. Someone is finally asking him to tell his story instead of filling in the gaps around him.
sessions.arc_notes: Largest org, least visibility. CDO org chart page was blank. Let him tell the story first -- don't lead with data because we don't have his team's data. Ask who from his team should talk to us on Tuesday/Wednesday.
top_questions: How do you allocate teams across the four Bitbucket workspaces? Do engineers move between madmobile, syscolabs, and madpayments, or are they siloed?
PR reviewer concentration -- are there individuals who review a disproportionate share of PRs? Who are the bottleneck reviewers?
Assignee concentration -- are there people with 50+ open issues assigned?
The Operations sub-team had a 57% L2 reduction and 72% L3 reduction in October 2025. How has that played out?

scheduling.why: Owns Enterprise Solutions (58 people) -- the largest eng org and the one with least visibility. No substitute.

sessions.notes: Still don't have names for his 58-person org. Chathura sending April 7. Ask Zubair who from his team to talk to for T6 or W1.

sessions.name: Akshay Bhasin
sessions.title: VP Payments Engineering
sessions.focus: Payments architecture, QE org structure, PCI, scaling
sessions.lead_with: Your workspace has the highest CI/CD coverage, cleanest architecture, and most modern stack in the company -- 87% CI/CD, TypeScript throughout, fully containerized on EKS, zero IAM users. I want to understand how you got there.
sessions.what_they_get: Recognition that his team has built the engineering quality benchmark. He should know that his codebase is the best in the company -- and that the rest of the org could learn from how he got there.
sessions.arc_notes: Lead with the quality benchmark observation. This is a conversation about what excellent looks like, not what's broken. Then explore the unusual QE structure and payments-specific challenges.
top_questions: The payments codebase has 87% CI/CD coverage, TypeScript throughout, domain-driven structure, EKS-native, 0 IAM users, Terraform state. Your team runs very differently from CAKE and Enterprise. Was that a deliberate engineering decision?
Restaurant QE -- 7 people -- reports to you, not Randy Brown. That's unusual -- the people testing restaurant software don't report to the person building it. How does that work?
The CDO roadmap shows 4+ Payments integration projects -- Sardine, RS2, CYBS, South State Bank. Plus 6+ Payments Ops projects. How do 9 R&D engineers cover that?
Payments had 19 incidents in the RCA data, most in 2021-2022. Peak was 26 incidents in 2022. Has reliability actually improved, or did the documentation practice stop?

relevant_pages.label: Payments Prod US -- EKS, zero EC2

scheduling.why: Owns Payments engineering (20+ people) including Restaurant QE. No substitute.

sessions.notes: Scope is larger than originally reported: Payments R&D (9), Restaurant QE (7), Biz Ops (4), PCI counterparts.

sessions.name: Matias Riglos
sessions.title: Solution Operations (DevOps/Infra)
sessions.focus: Infrastructure, CI/CD dual system, observability, cloud operations
topics: observability stack (Grafana/Mimir/Loki/Tempo vs Datadog vs Nagios/Munin)
artifact registries (CodeArtifact, NpmJS, Archiva, DockerHub, ECR)
AWS account structure and cost attribution

sessions.what_they_get: Someone who has already mapped the infrastructure and wants to understand his operational perspective, not just the architecture diagrams.
sessions.arc_notes: Fills the pre-lunch gap on Tuesday. He filed the AWS provisioning ticket (CLD-2431), handles Bitbucket invites, and is listed as a PCI DSS counterpart under Payments. Sits at the intersection of infra, security, and CI/CD.
top_questions: I count 6 observability tools in the stack: Grafana/Mimir/Loki/Tempo in Shared Services, Datadog for Concierge, Nagios and Munin from the Cake Engineering Tools page, Graylog, and DB Cacti. What's active vs legacy? Is there a consolidation roadmap?
Trend Micro Cloud One is costing $1,438/month in the CAKE Dev account. Is that intentional alongside Wiz?
CodeArtifact, NpmJS, Archiva, DockerHub, and ECR -- five artifact/registry services. Which are current?
The CAKE Dev account has 31 EC2 instances including 9 Jenkins servers totaling ~$15K/month. How much of that is tool infrastructure vs application workloads?

relevant_pages.label: AWS accounts, EC2, ECS, cost data

scheduling.why: Infrastructure and DevOps perspective -- CI/CD, observability, cloud ops.
alternatives: Matthew Griffin (Sr Cloud Engineer, Payments R&D) can cover infrastructure topics

sessions.notes: PCI DSS counterpart under Payments. Handles Bitbucket invites and AWS provisioning.

sessions.name: Chris Gomersall
sessions.title: Director Product Design
sessions.focus: Design process, PM/design/eng collaboration, design system
sessions.lead_with: I found an 'Agentic Lovable Dev Flow' document in Confluence -- Cursor + Chrome DevTools MCP for rapid UI prototyping. Is the design team experimenting with AI tooling too?
sessions.what_they_get: Recognition that design is part of the delivery system, not an afterthought. And curiosity about the AI tooling experiments his team is running.
sessions.arc_notes: Can go async if schedule is tight. If in-person, focus on how design capacity maps to engineering sprint cadence.
top_questions: How does design hand off to engineering? Is there a design system, or is each product line building its own components?
The Agentic Lovable Dev Flow document -- Cursor + Chrome DevTools MCP. How widespread is this beyond the Tesla team?

alternatives: Can go async (email/Teams follow-up) if unavailable

sessions.name: Chathura Ratnayake
sessions.title: CDO (Follow-up)
sessions.focus: Close gaps from Monday, triangulation against 15+ other conversations
topics: divergences between his account and his VPs'
sessions.lead_with: I've talked to your entire leadership team and several of their people over the last two days. A few things came up that I'd love your perspective on.
sessions.what_they_get: The triangulated picture -- what his org is saying that he may not be hearing. By this point you can offer real observations, not just extract information.
sessions.arc_notes: Questions generated from Tuesday synthesis. This is where the real depth happens. By now you've talked to Randy, Zubair, Akshay, Guilarte, EMs, ICs, and cross-functional partners. You know where stories diverge. The 48-hour gap since M1 gave him time to think about things that came up.
